title: Teaching
text:
Taught 300+ students over ~5 years, preparing them for highly competitive undergraduate admission exams (e.g., BUET and other top universities).
Covered core subjects including Mathematics and Physics.
Designed structured lessons, practice problems, and exam strategies tailored to competitive testing environments.
Mentored students individually to strengthen conceptual understanding and performance under time constraints.
Students successfully admitted to top institutions including BUET and other leading universities in Bangladesh.

title: Souvik Das
text:
I am a PhD student in Computer Science at Texas A&M University. My work is broadly in security, privacy-preserving machine learning, and high-performance computing. I am affiliated with the SPIES Lab and the HipGraph Lab.
My current research focuses on privacy-preserving Transformer inference (PPTI). I am interested in how secure inference workloads behave in practice, especially in terms of latency, communication overhead, ciphertext-operation costs, and hardware efficiency.
Research Interests: Privacy-Preserving Machine Learning, Cryptographic Inference Systems, Systems for Machine Learning, High-Performance Computing, Security and Privacy
Advisors:
Dr. Nitesh Saxena · Dr. Ariful Azad
Email: souvik.das@tamu.edu
Office: PETR 230 | PETR 246
CV · Google Scholar · GitHub · LinkedIn

title: Research
text:
My research lies at the intersection of security, high-performance computing, and machine learning systems, with a focus on privacy-preserving inference for modern AI models.
I study how cryptographic techniques—such as homomorphic encryption (HE) and secure multi-party computation (MPC)—can be used to enable secure inference on sensitive data, while still maintaining practical performance and scalability.
A key theme of my work is bridging theory and deployment: understanding not just whether secure inference is possible, but whether it is efficient, deployable, and systemically realistic. This includes analyzing trade-offs across: - latency and throughput
- communication overhead
- ciphertext packing and operation costs
- hardware-aware performance constraints

title: Research
text:
My research lies at the intersection of security, high-performance computing, and machine learning systems, with a focus on privacy-preserving inference for modern AI models.
I study how cryptographic techniques—such as homomorphic encryption (HE) and secure multi-party computation (MPC)—can be used to enable secure inference on sensitive data, while still maintaining practical performance and scalability.
A key theme of my work is bridging theory and deployment: understanding not just whether secure inference is possible, but whether it is efficient, deployable, and systemically realistic. This includes analyzing trade-offs across: - latency and throughput
- communication overhead
- ciphertext packing and operation costs
- hardware-aware performance constraints

title: Research
text:
Projects

Privacy-Preserving Transformer Inference (PPTI)

Investigating the systems and performance characteristics of secure Transformer inference pipelines.
Analyzing end-to-end latency, communication costs, and scaling behavior across different cryptographic designs.
Studying how architectural choices (e.g., attention mechanisms, packing strategies) affect real-world deployment feasibility.

Outcome: - Systematization of Knowledge (SoK) on evaluating privacy-preserving Transformer inference systems
- Development of a regime-aware evaluation perspective for fair and realistic comparisons



Encrypted ML Systems Prototyping (OpenFHE / CKKS)

Exploring how core ML operations behave under CKKS-based homomorphic encryption.
Implementing and benchmarking encrypted primitives such as:

linear layers

normalization

approximate nonlinear functions


Studying trade-offs between accuracy, latency, and ciphertext operations.

Focus: - Understanding practical bottlenecks in encrypted inference systems
- Building intuition for system-level optimization opportunities



Container Security: Malicious Code Injection Detection

Developed techniques for detecting malicious code injection in containerized environments.
Designed methods leveraging behavioral patterns and system-level monitoring.
Undergraduate thesis work supported by RISE Research Grant (BUET).

title: Souvik Das
text:
Research Summary
My recent work explores secure and privacy-preserving inference for Transformer models. I am particularly interested in the systems aspects of encrypted and cryptographic inference, including:

performance bottlenecks in privacy-preserving inference
communication vs. computation tradeoffs
hardware-aware evaluation of secure ML workloads
practical deployment challenges in cryptographic machine learning systems

title: Research
text:

Privacy-Preserving Machine Learning (PPML)
Cryptographic Inference Systems (HE, MPC)
Systems for Machine Learning
High-Performance Computing for AI
Security & Privacy in AI Deployment

title: Research
text:
Ongoing & Future Directions

Hardware-aware optimization of cryptographic ML systems

GPU-accelerated secure inference

Deployment-oriented evaluation frameworks for PPML systems

Bridging systems + cryptography + ML for real-world secure AI
